content:
First note that if \(n\) has \(d\) digits then 
\[
\text{(product of digits of }n)\;\le 9^d,
\]
whereas
\[
n^2-15n-27\approx n^2
\]
grows much faster than \(9^d\) once \(n\) exceeds about \(10^d\).  A quick case‐by‐case check shows:

1)  One‐digit \(n\le9\):  then product of digits = \(n\), so
   \[
     n=n^2-15n-27
     \;\Longrightarrow\;
     n^2-16n-27=0,
   \]
   which has no integer solutions in \(\{1,\dots,9\}.\)

2)  Two‐digit \(n=10a+b\) with \(1\le a\le9\), \(0\le b\le9\):
    One bounds \(n^2-15n-27\le81\) and checks \(n=10,\dots,99\).  
    In fact one finds the only solution is
    \[
      n=17,\quad 1\cdot7=7,\quad 17^2-15\cdot17-27=7.
    \]

3)  For three (or more) digits the quadratic \(n^2-15n-27\) already exceeds \(9^d\) before \(n\) even reaches \(10^{d-1}\), so no further solutions arise.

Hence the only positive integer solution is \(n=17\), and the requested sum is
\[
\boxed{17}.
\]
